trajectory_summary: A legitimacy anchor lands cleanly in the first 30 seconds, Juan self-discloses enthusiasm and a bounded spouse stall, Randy reframes the appointment as the wife-education venue, and the booking happens in under two minutes of real conversation. The call shape is textbook for short-form efficiency. The gap isn't in persuasion — it's in what got omitted: no bill capture, no verify depth, and a button-up missing four of five codex-required elements, leaving show-rate structural risk in an otherwise clean close.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: Nine or ten? Oh, yeah. That that's great. 10:10 is perfect.
primary_coaching_focus.why: After Juan confirms the time, Randy sends him off with none of the four codex-required show-rate anchors: no one-hour block statement, no phone/computer requirement, no no-driving instruction, no 'is that fair?' verbal handshake. The codex explicitly ties these — especially no_driving_at_computer and is_that_fair_handshake — to no-show rate. A customer who joins from his car or forgets the computer requirement is a wasted sit. Randy built the energy to get the booking; a 60-second button_up sequence protects it.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. It looks like we had a meeting set up with you to show you a solar design for the home. Is that something
key_moments.stewart_read: Randy doesn't argue the 'not interested' and doesn't apologize for the call. He pivots immediately to the prior record — 'we had a meeting set up' — which does two things: establishes legitimacy ('we have a real reason to be calling') and reframes Juan's confusion as a memory gap rather than a hard refusal. The move is strategy-aligned without being verbatim-scripted.

key_moments.quote: That was for solar. I did do some type of estimate last week, but I didn't know that was for solar panels.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the moment Randy's legitimacy anchor lands. Juan's own prior action validates the call — Randy didn't have to argue anything. Floor-teachable signal: when a rep leads with the prior record correctly, the customer often resolves their own skepticism out loud. The rep's job at that moment is to listen and confirm, not add to.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. I had a few guys come by with Iron Solar before, and and they told me how my house is positioned directly in sunlight. It is. I mean, it's on a Bitcoin lot and no trees blocking it. So it is kinda perfect for solar panels, but I ain't really liked about it yet.
key_moments.stewart_read: Juan volunteers that his site is competitor-visited, south-facing, tree-free, and that he personally believes it's 'kinda perfect for solar panels.' This is a dense enthusiasm signal — qualified site, competitor-pitched, and a buyer who is already self-sold. Randy acknowledges none of it specifically. 'Iron Solar' is most likely a competitor (possibly ION with a mispronunciation, but the safer read is a competitor given context). The closer needs to know Juan is walking in with a prior proposal in hand.

key_moments.quote: Okay. I mean, once I get her in advance, there'll be no problem. Yeah. Well, that's what this meeting is for is to kinda explain to you guys both how it works and, you know, what the benefits are.
key_moments.stewart_read: Protocol-compliant. Randy converts the wife-not-convinced concern into the reason to book rather than an obstacle to overcome. 'That's what this meeting is for' reframes the appointment as the wife's education venue — exactly the spouse_decision protocol move. The 'once I get her in advance, there'll be no problem' opener is slightly overconfident in tone (Randy can't guarantee the wife will be convinced), but it doesn't violate procedure and it lands — Juan immediately asks for the day and time.
